devanagari: श्रीकृष्णस्य उलूखले बन्धनम् । श्रीशुक उवाच । एकदा गृहदासीषु यशोदा नन्दगेहिनी । कर्मान्तरनियुक्तासु निर्ममन्थ स्वयं दधि ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīkṛṣṇasya ulūkhale bandhanam śrīśuka uvāca ekadā gṛhadāsīṣu yaśodā nandagehinī karmāntaraniyuktāsu nirmamantha svayaṃ dadhi

translation:
Śrī Śuka continued:
One day, when the female servants were directed to do some other work, Yaśodā, the wife of Nanda, began to churn the curds personally.

devanagari: यानि यानीह गीतानि तद्‍बालचरितानि च । दधिनिर्मन्थने काले स्मरन्ती तान्यगायत ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: yāni yānīha gītāni tad‍bālacaritāni ca dadhinirmanthane kāle smarantī tānyagāyata

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ smṛ class 1 verb], [vocative dual from √ smṛ class 1 verb], [accusative dual from √ smṛ class 1 verb]

translation: At the time of churning the curds, she recalled whatever acts of her child were sung there in Vraja and she herself sang them.

devanagari: क्षौमं वासः पृथुकटितटे । बिभ्रती सूत्रनद्धं । पुत्रस्नेहस्नुतकुचयुगं । जातकम्पं च सुभ्रूः । रज्ज्वाकर्षश्रमभुजचलत् । कङ्कणौ कुण्डले च । ...
english_devnagari: kṣaumaṃ vāsaḥ pṛthukaṭitaṭe bibhratī sūtranaddhaṃ putrasnehasnutakucayugaṃ jātakampaṃ ca subhrūḥ rajjvākarṣaśramabhujacalat kaṅkaṇau kuṇḍale ca svinnaṃ vaktraṃ kabaravigalan mālatī nirmamantha

translation: Wearing round her big waist (loins) a silk garment fastened with a zone, and with her pair of shaking breasts that were overflowing with milk from maternal affection for her son, with her ear-rings set in motion as well as her bangles moving about her forearms, fatigued with pulling the churning rope to and fro and her face bedewed with drops of perspiration, the handsome lady went on churning while jasmine flowers continued to drop from the braids of her hair.

devanagari: तां स्तन्यकाम आसाद्य मथ्नन्तीं जननीं हरिः । गृहीत्वा दधिमन्थानं न्यषेधत्प्रीतिमावहन् ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: tāṃ stanyakāma āsādya mathnantīṃ jananīṃ hariḥ gṛhītvā dadhimanthānaṃ nyaṣedhatprītimāvahan

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual], [nominative plural], [vocative dual], [vocative plural], [accusative dual], [accusative plural]

translation: Approaching his mother who was engaged in churning (the curds), Śrī Hari, desiring to suck her, caught hold of the churning rod, and awakening her maternal affection for him (by is gestures etc.), he stopped her churning.

devanagari: तमङ्कमारूढमपाययत् स्तनं । स्नेहस्नुतं सस्मितमीक्षती मुखम् । अतृप्तमुत्सृज्य जवेन सा ययौ । उत्सिच्यमाने पयसि त्वधिश्रिते ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: tamaṅkamārūḍhamapāyayat stanaṃ snehasnutaṃ sasmitamīkṣatī mukham atṛptamutsṛjya javena sā yayau utsicyamāne payasi tvadhiśrite

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ snu class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ snu class 2 verb]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: On his climbing up to her lap, she began to suckle him at her bosom overflowing with milk, through affection, and to gaze at his countenance which was beaming with smiles. But when the milk placed on the oven (for boiling) was about to overflow, she went in haste, leaving him alone, still unsatisfied.

devanagari: सञ्जातकोपः स्फुरितारुणाधरं । सन्दश्य दद्भिर्दधिमन्थभाजनम् । भित्त्वा मृषाश्रुर्दृषदश्मना रहो । जघास हैयङ्गवमन्तरं गतः ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: sañjātakopaḥ sphuritāruṇādharaṃ sandaśya dadbhirdadhimanthabhājanam bhittvā mṛṣāśrurdṛṣadaśmanā raho jaghāsa haiyaṅgavamantaraṃ gataḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: Flared up with indignation, he bit his quivering ruddy lips with his teeth and smashed the pot for churning curds with a piece of stone (used for grinding condiments etc. on a slab of stone). Shedding false tears, he entered the interior of the house and began to eat butter clandestinely.

devanagari: उत्तार्य गोपी सुशृतं पयः पुनः । प्रविश्य संदृश्य च दध्यमत्रकम् । भग्नं विलोक्य स्वसुतस्य कर्म । तज्जहास तं चापि न तत्र पश्यती ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: uttārya gopī suśṛtaṃ payaḥ punaḥ praviśya saṃdṛśya ca dadhyamatrakam bhagnaṃ vilokya svasutasya karma tajjahāsa taṃ cāpi na tatra paśyatī
translation: After getting down the (pot of) well-boiled milk, the Gopa-lady re-entered (the churning-room) only to find that pot of curds smashed. Knowing that to be the act of her son and failing to see him there, she just laughed (it away).

devanagari: उलूखलाङ्घ्रेरुपरि व्यवस्थितं । मर्काय कामं ददतं शिचि स्थितम् । हैयङ्गवं चौर्यविशङ्कितेक्षणं । निरीक्ष्य पश्चात् सुतमागमच्छनैः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: ulūkhalāṅghrerupari vyavasthitaṃ markāya kāmaṃ dadataṃ śici sthitam haiyaṅgavaṃ cauryaviśaṅkitekṣaṇaṃ nirīkṣya paścāt sutamāgamacchanaiḥ

translation: Observing that her son was standing firmly on the base of an overturned mortar, giving freely to a monkey the butter kept on a swing, and looking about with his eyes through caution and fear, lest somebody should detect the theft, she stealthily approached him from behind.

devanagari: तां आत्तयष्टिं प्रसमीक्ष्य सत्वरः । ततोऽवरुह्यापससार भीतवत् । गोप्यन्वधावन्न यमाप योगिनां । क्षमं प्रवेष्टुं तपसेरितं मनः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: tāṃ āttayaṣṭiṃ prasamīkṣya satvaraḥ tato gopyanvadhāvanna yamāpa yogināṃ kṣamaṃ praveṣṭuṃ tapaseritaṃ manaḥ

translation: Seeing his mother clearly with a rod in her hand, he hastily got down from the mortar and ran away as if in fear. The Gopa-lady pursued him, but failed to overtake him whom even the minds of yogins impelled and attuned through the force of penance (and concentration) do not reach (even though they are capable to do so.)

devanagari: अन्वञ्चमाना जननी बृहच्चलत् । श्रोणीभराक्रान्तगतिः सुमध्यमा । जवेन विस्रंसितकेशबन्धन । च्युतप्रसूनानुगतिः परामृशत् ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: anvañcamānā jananī bṛhaccalat śroṇībharākrāntagatiḥ sumadhyamā javena visraṃsitakeśabandhana cyutaprasūnānugatiḥ parāmṛśat
translation: Running after him, the beautiful mother (lit. mother of beautiful waist) whose speed was impeded by her bulky, moving hips and who was, as it were, followed by the flowers dropping from her braid of hair loosened by the speed (of her running), ultimately caught hold of him.

devanagari: कृतागसं तं प्ररुदन्तमक्षिणी । कर्षन्तमञ्जन्मषिणी स्वपाणिना । उद्वीक्षमाणं भयविह्वलेक्षणं । हस्ते गृहीत्वा भिषयन्त्यवागुरत् ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: kṛtāgasaṃ taṃ prarudantamakṣiṇī karṣantamañjanmaṣiṇī svapāṇinā udvīkṣamāṇaṃ bhayavihvalekṣaṇaṃ haste gṛhītvā bhiṣayantyavāgurat
translation: Holding by the hand, she threatened to beat Kṛṣṇa, who was crying for having committed that offence, and was rubbing his eyes with his hand whereby the collyrium (applied to his eyes) got spread all over and who was looking up (to his mother) with eyes bewildered and agitated with fear.

devanagari: त्यक्त्वा यष्टिं सुतं भीतं विज्ञायार्भकवत्सला । इयेष किल तं बद्धुं दाम्नातद्वीर्यकोविदा ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: tyaktvā yaṣṭiṃ sutaṃ bhītaṃ vijñāyārbhakavatsalā iyeṣa kila taṃ baddhuṃ dāmnātadvīryakovidā

translation: Perceiving that her son was terrified, Yaśodā, who was extremely affectionate to her child, threw away the stick. It is traditionally reported that being unable to comprehend the prowess of Kṛṣṇa, Yaśodā wished to bind him with a rope.

devanagari: न चान्तर्न बहिर्यस्य न पूर्वं नापि चापरम् । पूर्वापरं बहिश्चान्तः जगतो यो जगच्च यः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: na cāntarna bahiryasya na pūrvaṃ nāpi cāparam pūrvāparaṃ bahiścāntaḥ jagato yo jagacca yaḥ
translation: [1] In relation with him there is neither inside nor outside, neither before nor behind; but he is in front and at the back, outside and inside the universe; (nay) he is the universe itself. Presuming such unmanifest supra-sensuous Lord who had assumed a human form, to be her child, the cowherdwoman tried to bind him with a rope to the mortar, as one would tie down an ordinary human child.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Padaratnāvalī: There is nothing self-existent and self-dependent as he is everywhere. Since he exists from eternity to eternity nothing pre-exists him or outlasts him. He exists before and after the universe, and is immanent and transcendent to the world. He is the universe as everything is within his power.

devanagari: तं मत्वात्मजमव्यक्तं मर्त्यलिङ्गमधोक्षजम् । गोपिकोलूखले दाम्ना बबन्ध प्राकृतं यथा ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ matvātmajamavyaktaṃ martyaliṅgamadhokṣajam gopikolūkhale dāmnā babandha prākṛtaṃ yathā

translation: [1] In relation with him there is neither inside nor outside, neither before nor behind; but he is in front and at the back, outside and inside the universe; (nay) he is the universe itself. Presuming such unmanifest supra-sensuous Lord who had assumed a human form, to be her child, the cowherdwoman tried to bind him with a rope to the mortar, as one would tie down an ordinary human child.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Padaratnāvalī: There is nothing self-existent and self-dependent as he is everywhere. Since he exists from eternity to eternity nothing pre-exists him or outlasts him. He exists before and after the universe, and is immanent and transcendent to the world. He is the universe as everything is within his power.

devanagari: तद्दाम बध्यमानस्य स्वार्भकस्य कृतागसः । द्व्यङ्गुलोनमभूत्तेन सन्दधेऽन्यच्च गोपिका ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: taddāma badhyamānasya svārbhakasya kṛtāgasaḥ dvyaṅgulonamabhūttena sandadhe

translation: While binding her child with a rope for his mischief, the cowherd lady found that it was short by two-fingers (about an inch or so). So she added to it another piece (of rope).

devanagari: यदाऽसीत् तदपि न्यूनं तेनान्यदपि सन्दधे । तदपि द्व्यङ्गुलं न्यूनं यद् यद् आदत्त बन्धनम् ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: yadā tadapi dvyaṅgulaṃ nyūnaṃ yad yad ādatta bandhanam

translation: When that (additional) string fell short, she tied yet another piece. In this way, whatever additional piece (of string) she brought, it too fell short by two fingers (about an inch).

devanagari: एवं स्वगेहदामानि यशोदा सन्दधत्यपि । गोपीनां सुस्मयन्तीनां स्मयन्ती विस्मिताभवत् ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ svagehadāmāni yaśodā sandadhatyapi gopīnāṃ susmayantīnāṃ smayantī vismitābhavat

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative dual from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative dual from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: While Yaśodā was putting together all the ropes in her house (and still it fell short by two fingers), all the Gopa- women laughed; and she too joined them smiling, but felt amazed at it.

devanagari: स्वमातुः स्विन्नगात्राया विस्रस्तकबरस्रजः । दृष्ट्वा परिश्रमं कृष्णः कृपयाऽसीत् स्वबन्धने ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: svamātuḥ svinnagātrāyā visrastakabarasrajaḥ dṛṣṭvā pariśramaṃ kṛṣṇaḥ kṛpayā
translation: Noticing the over-exhaustion of his mother whose body was bathed in perspiration, and the wreaths of flowers from whose loosened braids of hair were falling down, Kṛṣṇa, out of compassion for her, allowed himself to be tied.

devanagari: एवं सन्दर्शिता ह्यङ्ग हरिणा भृत्यवश्यता । स्ववशेनापि कृष्णेन यस्येदं सेश्वरं वशे ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ sandarśitā hyaṅga hariṇā bhṛtyavaśyatā svavaśenāpi kṛṣṇena yasyedaṃ seśvaraṃ vaśe
translation: In this way, dear Parīkṣit! was demonstrated his subjection to the control of his votaries by Hari, even though Kṛṣṇa is absolutely self-dependent and has the whole of the universe along with its rulers, under his control.

devanagari: नेमं विरिञ्चो न भवो न श्रीरप्यङ्गसंश्रया । प्रसादं लेभिरे गोपी यत् तत्प्राप विमुक्तिदात् ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: nemaṃ viriñco na bhavo na śrīrapyaṅgasaṃśrayā prasādaṃ lebhire gopī yat tatprāpa vimuktidāt

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: Neither (his son) god Brahmā nor (his very Soul) Lord Śiva nor the goddess Lakṣmī who clings to his person, was recipient of such grace as was enjoyed by the Gopi (Yaśodā) at the hands of that bestower of Mokṣa (Liberation).

devanagari: नायं सुखापो भगवान् देहिनां गोपिकासुतः । ज्ञानिनां चात्मभूतानां यथा भक्तिमतां इह ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: nāyaṃ sukhāpo bhagavān dehināṃ gopikāsutaḥ jñānināṃ cātmabhūtānāṃ yathā bhaktimatāṃ iha
translation: The glorious Lord Kṛṣṇa who is (i.e. has assumed the form of) the son of Yaśodā is not so easily attainable to the ascetics who identify themselves with their bodies and to those who possess spiritual wisdom, as to those who are devoted to him and identify themselves with him.[2]
purport:
Footnote [2]:
v.l. ātma-potānām / Who make him their only raft to cross Saṃsāra—Bhāgavata Candrikā, Padaratnāvalī

devanagari: कृष्णस्तु गृहकृत्येषु व्यग्रायां मातरि प्रभुः । अद्राक्षीत् अर्जुनौ पूर्वं गुह्यकौ धनदात्मजौ ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: kṛṣṇastu gṛhakṛtyeṣu vyagrāyāṃ mātari prabhuḥ adrākṣīt arjunau pūrvaṃ guhyakau dhanadātmajau
translation: While his mother Yaśodā was engrossed in carrying out her household duties, Lord Kṛṣṇa noticed that the pair of Arjuna trees were in their former birth, two Guhyakas, the sons of Kubera, the bestower of wealth.

devanagari: पुरा नारदशापेन वृक्षतां प्रापितौ मदात् । नलकूवरमणिग्रीवौ इति ख्यातौ श्रियान्वितौ ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: purā nāradaśāpena vṛkṣatāṃ prāpitau madāt nalakūvaramaṇigrīvau iti khyātau śriyānvitau

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active third dual], [imperative middle third single]
[nominative dual from √ khyā class 2 verb], [vocative dual from √ khyā class 2 verb], [accusative dual from √ khyā class 2 verb]

translation:
They were known as Nala Kūbara and Maṇigrīva. Endowed with great splendour as they were, they were reduced to the state of trees, through the curse imprecated by Nārada due to their pride.
